publications.title: Structural Mechanism Underpinning Cross-reactivity of a CD8+ T-cell Clone That Recognizes a Peptide Derived from Human Telomerase Reverse Transcriptase.
publications.authors: Cole DK, van den Berg HA, Lloyd A, Crowther MD, Beck K, Ekeruche-Makinde J, Miles JJ, Bulek AM, Dolton G, Schauenburg AJ, Wall A, Fuller A, Clement M, Laugel B, Rizkallah PJ, Wooldridge L, Sewell AK.
publications.executive_summary: The study examines the ILA1 CD8+ T-cell clone, which recognizes a peptide derived from human telomerase reverse transcriptase (hTERT) presented on MHC-I. Structural analysis reveals that despite making broad contacts across spatially distant peptide residues rather than focusing on a minimal motif, the ILA1 TCR remains cross-reactive—a finding that challenges the prevailing model linking focused binding to high degeneracy. The TCR exhibits position-specific tolerance, with lower flexibility at directly contacted or adjacent residues and greater tolerance elsewhere, suggesting that cross-reactivity is controlled by the distribution of functional TCR-peptide contacts rather than by binding geometry alone. These findings illuminate how TCRs balance specific recognition with cross-reactivity, with implications for understanding both beneficial immunity to pathogens and unwanted responses in autoimmunity and transplant rejection.
